author: @Rob-Riot
text:
I have been using and rewinding microwave transformers for years in radio linear amplifier projects, both for high voltage supplies by lifting the grounded end of the HV winding, and also by removing the HV winding and rewiring with heavy gage wire for filament supplies. Many of these transformers are capable of 1 amp of current at 2000+ volts which is a substantial amount of current.
 The only downside is sometimes they can be mechanically noisy, and are not as efficient as transformers that are designed with more conventional core lamination and winding principle's. The upside is cost! They are dirt cheap compared to commercially available equivalents designed for this application

author: @jamescampbell7823
text: Very nice work.  I've done quite a few of these myself, I've got quite the box full of them now. What I do is,  place it in a vice with the bottom of the "M" facing up, That's the part that's welded on. The welds are very shallow and I use an angle grinder to grind them off. The bottom section comes off then. Then I put it down on a workbench, with the side I removed facing down. I use pieces of wood, about 2"X2" and 6" long to tap down both sides of the wire windings, a few taps on one side and a few taps on the other, you can grease the metal a little bit to help it slide down. I generally remove the primary winding because I use the secondary winding in place and cut out the center section up to the winding and make electromagnets out of them. If you're going to make an electromagnet out of these (and they make excellent ones!) Then you want to cut out the center section so that you're left with a u-shape which is the best for a magnet.

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text: As these are often free MOTs, the safe bet is to scrap the primary and rewind, or find another transformer.  That being said, depending upon the extent and location of the nicked wires you can apply a liberal portion of resin to the windings and let it cure up thus re-insulating the wires.  The enamel coating and follow-up resin coating (post construction) performed by the manufacturer are done under a vacuum to pull the resin into and between the windings ensuring a better coverage.  If you have this option, and you must absolutely use the damaged primary, I'd say resin it up and place under a vacuum while it is curing.  I'd advise finding another MOT however, as you don't need to muck about with all of that and risk your safety.  I like to use a 2mm copper strip as a blade stop.  Bend it into a U and place it over the primary side (upside down) that you're working on, and throw a ziptie or large hose clamp around it to hold it snug.  Takes a hair bit longer but it'll serve as training wheels until you get the feel of cutting them as Robert shows.  Best of luck in future scrapping

author: @mortneff4674
text: OK just curious, Y take the small winding out instead of using them? I understand the idea of the heavy wire for high draw stuff but Y other than that?

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text: Depends on your desired use.   You can use it as is by reversing the connections - this is even allowed under code depending upon the voltages used and Txfmr KVA rating and configuration.  Delta-Wye to Wye-Delta, for example, but only up to around 3.5KVA.  There are several issues caused by this which you need to be aware of.  Because the core is being magnetized from the inside out when reversed, the excitation inrush can be up to 23 times higher, making it difficult to coordinate with mains breaker ratings.  MOST IMPORTANTLY - in ALL MOTs that I've used or seen, one side of the secondary is connected to the transformer's core.  To energize it in reverse would mean connecting mains neutral to the core, thus the neutral is live under certain fault conditions.  This must be disconnected and left 'free floating'.  The HV side shunt is there to prevent oversaturation of the core's field and limit the current to the magnetron.  If you just remove the shunt, the air gap still produces leakage inductance.  When Robert removes the secondary and rewinds it, the shunt is removed but the air gap is also removed, allowing you to configure your desired output voltage and keeping the transformer from heating as much.   These are the main reasons that it's performed in the manner Robert does.  Hope that helps answer your question!  Even if the answer is 10 months delayed :P

author: @ebenwaterman5858
text:
I've been trying to find out what those core pieces between the primary and secondary windings do. They show at 3:45 in the vid on the "really big one".   They seem to be a magnetic shunt of some sort. Do you know what they do? should I remove them? They are also visible later in the xformer you used to melt the bolt.
p.s. Nice lesson, thanx. :)

replies.author: @brianspencer6397
replies.text:
They are indeed magnetic shunts. Their purpose is to bypass the magnetic flux created in the primary (mains) coil as the load on the transformer increases, lowering the output voltage, and limiting the current that the load can draw. They are needed in a microwave as the magnetron takes several thousand volts to 'strike' it and get it started, but it runs on several hundred volts once the arc is established inside the magnetron. This also limits the available power delivery to whatever the rating of the magnetron is; without them, the magnetron would probably draw excess power once it 'struck', and could either destroy itself, or overload the transformer. (The shunts have little effect on no load, as the magnetic field prefers to flow thru the much larger cross section main core.)

Leaving them in will therefore limit the output voltage and current available on load from the transformer, and reduce the voltage out as load increases, which is NOT what a spot welder needs. Removing them will allow the transformer to deliver the maximum power to the load, and will help to keep the output voltage (relatively) constant. The 'old' style arc welders with the big knob use a moveable shunt to control the available current, (and reduce the arc voltage,) during welding, but the shunt had no effect on the unloaded voltage, so it was always easy to strike the arc.

replies.author: @fromagefrizzbizz9377
replies.text:
The turns ratio here is about 100:1.  Feeding it backwards would produce 22Kv, which is generally about 20 times higher than the magnet wire insulation can stand.  That said, hooking a 2 turn transformer to 220V with practical inductances at 50-60hz is going to blow your breaker almost instantly it might as well be a dead short.

If you want to try it, wear body armor and a face shield, and flip the switch with a long wooden stick.

replies.author: @jakeblanton6853
replies.text: @fromagefrizzbizz9377 -- You could easily protect the circuit so that the breaker is not kicked by adding an incandescent light bulb in series with the primary... You definitely need more insulation on the wires for that sort of voltage though...

replies.author: @fromagefrizzbizz9377
replies.text: @jakeblanton6853 Putting a resistor (which is essentially what an incandescent light bulb is in this scenario) will reduce the input voltage and hence the output voltage, in this case a *lot*, and would vary all over the place depending on load.  Putting a resistor in series with a transformer used to step up or down voltage usually means you're making a mistake.

replies.author: @jakeblanton6853
replies.text: @fromagefrizzbizz9377 -- Yeah, there's always compromises with any design decision... If you want to eliminate the possibility of blowing the circuity breaker though, using a light bulb as a current limiting resistor will work, but you will also be decreasing the power that the transformer can subsequently put out... If you were getting 22KV out of it, then you would need spark plug wire insulation type thicknesses...

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text: @jakeblanton6853 The reason why Fromage mentions the breaker dilemma is because the inrush excitation is much higher when reversed since the core is being magnetized from the inside out.  Loaded or not.  And steps need to be made when doing this to free float and  'corner ground' the neutral, as it's connected on the output side (in original use) to the core.  When reversed the neutral becomes live under several fault conditions and is not advised.  Corner grounding - That involves significantly more danger and steps to ensure it's done properly, and there are a whole host of differential voltage readings and requirements that must be undertaken from the device all the way back to the service utility.

author: @RWBHere
text:
I worked in a transformer, choke and inductor making factory for a while. We made devices so that they didn't vibrate and make noise, to improve insulation performance, and to improve efficiency. Some of the final construction steps were to immerse the devices in a bath of varnish, in a vacuum chamber. This sucked the varnish between the core laminations, the bobbins, and all of the coil windings, when the vacuum was released. Then they were drained and baked in an oven to harden the varnish. A similar process is used worldwide, using other varnish or wax.

The downside is that transformers treated in those ways are very difficult to dismantle nondestructively. We often had older transformers, made by many different manufacturers, sent in for reverse-engineering and rebuild. That could be done least destructively by immersing a device in solvent for a couple of days, and then its core could be dismantled. The first 'E' or 'T' (for either 'E-I' or 'C-T' laminations) of the transformer or choke core would be drifted out with a thin drift, and then the dismantling became progressively easier. Bobbins could then be unwound, measured and turns counted, before being cleaned and rebuilt.

In the light of this, your destructive method is perhaps the easiest one to adopt, if a winding can be sacrificed.

replies.author: @kurtilingus
replies.text: Im sure they were plenty warm, but as far as the bolt, it's a relative bottleneck in between two other conductors with a likewise relatively/proportionally much, much lower resistance. For that bolt to "keep up", and it clearly can't, it must dissappate a wholllllee lot of that excess energy screaming  through it faster than it's able to handle gracefully as heat. Lots and lots of heat. You'd get *exact* same effect if you were to have those same grips connected to some ~4AWG from the transformer, no bolt and just touching *them* together. Different magnitude, same relative bottleneck.

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text: It's more resistive - remember V=IR.  Current and resistance are directly proportional, increase the resistance and the current will increase.  In this case it's localized in a smaller cross-sectional area at a higher resistive value = molten metal lava.

replies.author: @blakeireland1533
replies.text: you would work out the voltage by either measuring it or using the formula n1/v1 = n2/v2 where n1 is the number of turns on the primary, v1 is the input voltage, n2 is the number of turns on the secondary, and v2 is the number you are trying to calculate, output voltage. Once you have the output voltage, you can either find the rated wattage on a label or calculate it using p=iv where p is watts, i is current, and v is volts, and then to calculate the output current you can simply rearrange that equation into i=p/v and plug in your calculated wattage and output voltage to get your max possible output current

replies.author: @brianspencer6397
replies.text:
A rule of thumb for transformers is the power out is equal to power in (give or take a bit.) A rough figure for output current can be found by noting the mains voltage and current on the label of the microwave. A 220 volt, 5 amp microwave has a transformer rated at roughly (220 volts x 5 amps) or 1100 watts. If your output voltage from a new coil is (say) 10 volts, then the available current on the load side is (1100 watts / 10 volts) or 110 amps. If you plan on pushing a transformer to near its maximum ratings, (a) put a thermal cutout* in the mains circuit, and include a fan to provide cooling, or the transformer may overheat and burn out.

*These are often included in the microwave oven, mounted on the magnetron cooling fins, and also sometimes on the outside of the cooking chamber.

replies.text: What you are talking about is called cascading. And no, if you plug your average MOT into mains and cascade it into another, the primary winding would not be able to handle it by any means.

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text:
It does depend on a couple of factors, including the dielectric strength rating of the enameled copper also known as the breakdown voltage, but connecting them in series will be doable generally as you're increasing the voltage and keeping the current the same.  Most MOTs output in the 2.3Kv range.  You can determine the dielectric strength of wire you're using by measuring the thickness of the coated wire, and the thickness of a portion that the enamel has been removed from.  You would also need to know the type of coating that was applied.  The temperature rating is also a factor in the wire Grading (1-3).  I pulled this calculation from the EE stack exchange recently for a project:

Breakdown voltage depends on the thickness of the covering, which can be of 3 types: Grade 1, Grade 2 and Grade 3. Higher grades have thicker insulation and thus higher breakdown voltages.

The temperature class indicates the temperature of the wire where it has a 20,000 hour service life. At lower temperatures the service life of the wire is longer (about a factor 2 for every 10 °C lower temperature). Common temperature classes are 105° C, 130° C, 155° C, 180° C and 220° C."

Calculation of breakdown voltage

The breakdown voltage depends mainly on the thickness of the insulation (see formula below), but also on the bare wire diameter, the application temperature of the coil and the type of enamel.

Calculation of average values Ds (dielectric strength):

Ds = t x Vμ [Volt], with

Ds : breakdown voltage

T : increase due to insulation,

t = da – dnom, : wire diameters with and without insulation

Vμm = volts per micron insulation (dependent on type of insulation)

Example:

Test with cylindrical electrode (round wire)

dnom 0.071mm (bare wire nominal diameter)

da = 0.083mm (wire with coating)

t = da – dnom = 0.083 – 0.071 = 0.012mm = 12μm (thickness of insulation between wires)

Vμ = 205 V/μm, therefore

Ds = 12μ x 205 V/μ = 2,460 V

The insulation between turns is also less of a factor than the insulation between the turns and the transformer core. You can always apply another coating of resin to the transformers, but keep in mind this will limit it's ability to dispel heat, shortening the lifespan of the device.

author: @barry2718
text:
Great video. The "shims" you refereed to are actually magnetic shunts. Their purpose is to provide a magnetic field path when there is a short circuit on the secondary. Normal transformers when the secondary shorts out the inductance  drops to zero and so the primary current increases without limit until a fuse blows or the primary dc resistance ( copper wire resistance) limits the maximum primary current. With a reactive or magnetic shunt in place the primary still sees some inductance ( inductive reactance) and therefore is current limited. I would put the shims back in when making a low voltage welding transformer so that under secondary short circuit you don't overload your mains supply and keep blowing fuses.

Have a read of this . https://www.quora.com/What-is-the-purpose-of-the-magnetic-shunt-that-is-in-most-microwave-power-transformers-Can-the-shunt-be-safely-removed-when-winding-a-single-replacement-secondary

You can do some bad stuff with them also! 
I damaged the primary windings on one, I actually tried to use by drilling, then knocking the winding out, a drill press would work much better, mine was epoxied in also, I was making a spot welder, I don't think there was enough windings, and voltage was too low, I ended up using lithium batteries, when the voltage gets low, it acts like the transformer, it 1-1.5 turns of 10 gauge copper, about 20+ 10 gauge wires in a bunch, it probably had all the amps, but it seems the voltage needs to be high enough to penetrate the metal, both layers, below 10.5v it doesn't weld well. Although with a long enough pulse, the metal will flash red, but no weld, but with higher voltage, short pulse, it welds nice, I think with about 5-6 turns, of about 2-4 gauge wire it would work fine,  great video!

replies.text: No - the currents in the line wire and the return wire inside the cord are equal and in opposite directions, so their magnetic fields will be equal and opposite, and will cancel out. (Google 'non-inductive resistor'.) Resistive heating only in extension cords.

I knew an electrician a few years back who liked to coil up about 2 feet of extra live and neutral wire (separately) in the ceiling when he installed lights - on every single can light, and he called them 'service loops.'
